NCT02551094.name: COLCOT

allOutcomes.title: Composite (CV Death, Cardiac Arrest, MI, Stroke, Urgent Revasc)

NCT02551094.snippet: Source: Tardif JC et al. NEJM 2019;381:2497-2505.

evidence.label: Enrollment & Randomization
evidence.source: Tardif JC et al. NEJM 2019;381:2497-2505, CONSORT
evidence.text: A total of 4,745 patients who had had a recent myocardial infarction (within 30 days) were randomized: 2,366 to colchicine 0.5 mg daily and 2,379 to matching placebo. Median follow-up was 22.6 months.

evidence.label: Primary Composite Endpoint
evidence.source: Tardif JC et al. NEJM 2019;381:2497-2505, Table 2
evidence.text: The primary endpoint (cardiovascular death, resuscitated cardiac arrest, myocardial infarction, stroke, or urgent hospitalization for angina leading to coronary revascularization) occurred in 131 patients (5.5%) in the colchicine group and 170 patients (7.1%) in the placebo group (hazard ratio, 0.77; 95% CI, 0.61 to 0.96; P=0.02).

evidence.label: Risk of Bias Assessment (RoB 2.0, 5 Domains)
evidence.source: Tardif JC et al. NEJM 2019;381:2497-2505; NCT02551094
evidence.text: D1 Randomization: LOW — computer-generated randomization with stratification by site. D2 Deviations: LOW — double-blind with matching placebo. D3 Missing Data: LOW — vital status ascertained for 99.5%. D4 Outcome Measurement: LOW — independent blinded clinical events committee. D5 Selection of Reported Result: LOW — pre-specified primary composite; ITT analysis.

evidence.label: Secondary Outcomes
evidence.source: Tardif JC et al. NEJM 2019;381:2497-2505, Table 2

evidence.label: Anti-Inflammatory Mechanism
evidence.source: Tardif JC et al. NEJM 2019; Leung YY et al. J Clin Pharmacol 2015;55:1093-1107
evidence.text: Colchicine inhibits tubulin polymerization, disrupting neutrophil chemotaxis, adhesion, and inflammasome activation (NLRP3). At low doses (0.5 mg/day), it selectively targets inflammation without immunosuppression, reducing IL-1β, IL-6, and CRP. This anti-inflammatory action on the atherosclerotic plaque is hypothesized to reduce plaque instability and clinical events.

evidence.label: Eligibility (AACT-derived)
evidence.source: AACT 2026-04-12 eligibilities.txt
evidence.text: AACT-derived (auto-populated 2026-05-04). Sex: ALL; min age 18 Years; healthy volunteers: f. CRITERIA: "Inclusion Criteria:~* Males and females of at least 18 years of age capable and willing to provide informed consent~* Patient must have suffered a documented acute myocardial infarction within the last 30 days~* Patient must be treated according to national guidelines (including anti-platelet therapy, statin, renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitor (preferably angiotensin-converting enzyme) and beta-blocker when indicated)~* Patient must have completed any planned percutaneous revascular...

ACTRN-LODOCO2.name: LoDoCo2

allOutcomes.title: Composite (CV Death, Spontaneous MI, Ischaemic Stroke, Ischaemia-Driven Revasc)

ACTRN-LODOCO2.snippet: Source: Nidorf SM et al. NEJM 2020;383:1838-1847.

evidence.label: Enrollment & Randomization
evidence.source: Nidorf SM et al. NEJM 2020;383:1838-1847, CONSORT
evidence.text: A total of 5,522 patients with chronic coronary disease (documented by CT coronary angiography or invasive angiography or coronary artery calcium score ≥400 Agatston units) were randomized: 2,762 to colchicine 0.5 mg daily and 2,760 to matching placebo. Median follow-up was 28.6 months.

evidence.label: Primary Composite Endpoint
evidence.source: Nidorf SM et al. NEJM 2020;383:1838-1847, Table 2
evidence.text: The primary endpoint (cardiovascular death, spontaneous myocardial infarction, ischaemic stroke, or ischaemia-driven coronary revascularization) occurred in 187 patients (6.8%) in the colchicine group and 264 patients (9.6%) in the placebo group (hazard ratio, 0.69; 95% CI, 0.57 to 0.83; P<0.001).

evidence.label: Risk of Bias Assessment (RoB 2.0, 5 Domains)
evidence.source: Nidorf SM et al. NEJM 2020;383:1838-1847; ANZCTR
evidence.text: D1 Randomization: LOW — computer-generated block randomization stratified by site. D2 Deviations: LOW — double-blind, placebo-controlled. D3 Missing Data: LOW — follow-up complete for vital status in >99%. D4 Outcome Measurement: LOW — independent blinded endpoint adjudication committee. D5 Selection of Reported Result: LOW — primary composite pre-specified; ITT analysis.

evidence.label: Secondary Outcomes
evidence.source: Nidorf SM et al. NEJM 2020;383:1838-1847, Table 2

evidence.label: Run-In Phase Design
evidence.source: Nidorf SM et al. NEJM 2020;383:1838-1847
evidence.text: LoDoCo2 included a 30-day open-label run-in phase to identify patients intolerant of colchicine (primarily GI side effects). Of 6,528 patients who entered run-in, 5,522 (84.6%) tolerated the drug and were randomized. This design may reduce adverse event rates in the randomized phase but limits generalizability to colchicine-tolerant patients.

ACTRN-COPS.name: COPS

allOutcomes.title: MACE (All-Cause Death, ACS, Ischaemia-Driven Revasc, Non-Cardioembolic Stroke)

ACTRN-COPS.snippet: Source: Tong DC et al. Circulation 2020;142:1890-1900.

evidence.label: Enrollment & Randomization
evidence.source: Tong DC et al. Circulation 2020;142:1890-1900
evidence.text: A total of 795 patients with acute coronary syndrome (STEMI, NSTEMI, or unstable angina) requiring hospitalization were randomized: 396 to colchicine (0.5 mg BID for 1 month, then 0.5 mg daily for 11 months) and 399 to placebo. Median follow-up was 12 months.

evidence.label: Primary Composite Endpoint
evidence.source: Tong DC et al. Circulation 2020;142:1890-1900, Table 2
evidence.text: The primary endpoint (all-cause death, ACS, ischaemia-driven urgent revascularization, or non-cardioembolic stroke at 12 months) occurred in 24 patients (6.1%) in the colchicine group and 38 patients (9.5%) in the placebo group (HR 0.65; 95% CI, 0.38 to 1.09; P=0.10).

evidence.label: Risk of Bias Assessment (RoB 2.0, 5 Domains)
evidence.source: Tong DC et al. Circulation 2020;142:1890-1900
evidence.text: D1 Randomization: LOW — computer-generated block randomization. D2 Deviations: SOME CONCERNS — higher rate of GI side effects may have caused partial unblinding. D3 Missing Data: LOW — complete follow-up. D4 Outcome Measurement: LOW — blinded endpoint adjudication. D5 Selection of Reported Result: LOW — pre-specified primary composite.

evidence.label: Mortality Signal
evidence.source: Tong DC et al. Circulation 2020;142:1890-1900, Table 2
evidence.text: CAUTION: All-cause mortality was numerically higher in the colchicine group: 8 (2.0%) vs 1 (0.3%), HR 8.20 (1.03-65.6). Five of 8 deaths in the colchicine group were non-cardiovascular (2 pneumonia, 2 sepsis, 1 metastatic cancer). This imbalance was not observed in larger trials (COLCOT, LoDoCo2).

NCT03048825.name: CLEAR SYNERGY

allOutcomes.title: Composite (CV Death, MI, Stroke, Ischaemia-Driven Revasc)

NCT03048825.snippet: Source: Jolly SS et al. NEJM 2025;392:633-642.

evidence.label: Enrollment & Randomization
evidence.source: Jolly SS et al. NEJM 2025;392:633-642, CONSORT
evidence.text: Within a 2x2 factorial design (colchicine vs placebo; spironolactone vs placebo), 7,062 patients with myocardial infarction undergoing PCI were randomized to the colchicine comparison: 3,528 to colchicine 0.5 mg daily and 3,534 to placebo. Median follow-up was 3.0 years.

evidence.label: Primary Composite Endpoint
evidence.source: Jolly SS et al. NEJM 2025;392:633-642, Table 2
evidence.text: The primary endpoint (cardiovascular death, recurrent myocardial infarction, stroke, or unplanned ischaemia-driven revascularization) occurred in 322 patients (9.1%) in the colchicine group and 327 patients (9.3%) in the placebo group (hazard ratio, 0.99; 95% CI, 0.85 to 1.16; P=0.93).

evidence.label: Risk of Bias Assessment (RoB 2.0, 5 Domains)
evidence.source: Jolly SS et al. NEJM 2025;392:633-642; NCT03048825
evidence.text: D1 Randomization: LOW — central interactive web response system with stratification. D2 Deviations: LOW — double-blind, double-dummy, placebo-controlled. D3 Missing Data: LOW — vital status ascertained for 99.4%. D4 Outcome Measurement: LOW — independent blinded clinical events committee. D5 Selection of Reported Result: LOW — pre-specified primary composite; ITT analysis.

evidence.label: Factorial Design Context
evidence.source: Jolly SS et al. NEJM 2025;392:633-642
evidence.text: CLEAR SYNERGY used a 2x2 factorial design simultaneously testing colchicine vs placebo AND spironolactone vs placebo. No significant interaction was observed between the two treatments. The colchicine comparison was negative (HR 0.99, P=0.93), the largest null result in the colchicine-CVD evidence base.

evidence.label: Eligibility (AACT-derived)
evidence.source: AACT 2026-04-12 eligibilities.txt
evidence.text: AACT-derived (auto-populated 2026-05-04). Sex: ALL; min age 18 Years; healthy volunteers: f. CRITERIA: "Inclusion Criteria:~1. a) Patients with STEMI referred for PCI within 12 hours of symptom onset, have a culprit lesion amenable to stenting, and with planned SYNERGY stent implantation for SYNERGY registry~   OR~   b) Patients with STEMI referred for PCI within 48 hours of symptom onset, not prospectively enrolled in SYNERGY stent registry~   OR~   c) Patients with diagnosis of Non STEMI with ischemic symptoms and either Hs Troponin > or = 300x ULN or Troponin > or = 200x ULN who have undergo...

NCT02898610.name: CONVINCE

allOutcomes.title: Composite (Recurrent Ischaemic Stroke, MI, Cardiac Arrest, Hospitalisation for Unstable Angina)

NCT02898610.snippet: Source: Kelly PJ et al. Lancet 2024;404:125-133.

evidence.label: Enrollment & Randomization
evidence.source: Kelly PJ et al. Lancet 2024;404:125-133
evidence.text: A total of 3,144 patients with non-cardioembolic ischaemic stroke or high-risk TIA were randomized: 1,569 to colchicine 0.5 mg daily plus usual care and 1,575 to usual care alone. Median follow-up was 33.6 months. This was an open-label trial (PROBE design).

evidence.label: Primary Composite Endpoint
evidence.source: Kelly PJ et al. Lancet 2024;404:125-133, Table 2
evidence.text: The primary endpoint (first occurrence of recurrent ischaemic stroke, myocardial infarction, cardiac arrest, or hospitalisation for unstable angina) occurred in 153 patients (9.8%) in the colchicine group and 185 patients (11.7%) in the usual-care group (hazard ratio, 0.84; 95% CI, 0.68 to 1.05; P=0.12).

evidence.label: Risk of Bias Assessment (RoB 2.0, 5 Domains)
evidence.source: Kelly PJ et al. Lancet 2024;404:125-133
evidence.text: D1 Randomization: LOW — central web-based randomization. D2 Deviations: SOME CONCERNS — open-label design (PROBE); however, blinded endpoint adjudication mitigates performance bias. D3 Missing Data: LOW — complete follow-up for primary endpoint. D4 Outcome Measurement: LOW — blinded endpoint adjudication committee. D5 Selection of Reported Result: LOW — pre-specified primary composite; ITT analysis.

evidence.label: Open-Label Design Consideration
evidence.source: Kelly PJ et al. Lancet 2024;404:125-133
evidence.text: CONVINCE used a Prospective, Randomised, Open-label, Blinded Endpoint (PROBE) design. This open-label approach is a limitation: patients and treating physicians knew treatment allocation. However, endpoint adjudication was blinded, and the primary outcome was objective (stroke, MI, death). The trial was stopped early for funding reasons (pandemic delays), reducing statistical power.

evidence.label: Eligibility (AACT-derived)
